text: What is the output of `[] == ![]`?
feedback: `![]` evaluates to `false`. The comparison becomes `[] == false`. `[]` coerces to `""` (empty string), which coerces to `0`. `false` coerces to `0`. `0 == 0` is `true`.

text: What does `3 > 2 > 1` evaluate to?
feedback: Operators evaluate left-to-right. `3 > 2` evaluates to `true`. Then, `true > 1` evaluates to `1 > 1`, which is `false`.

text: What is the output of `console.log(typeof typeof 1);`?
feedback: `typeof 1` evaluates to the string `"number"`. Then, `typeof "number"` evaluates to `"string"`.
By default, `.sort()` converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code units. `"15"` comes before `"2"`.
Inside `b`, `function a() {}` is hoisted to the top of `b`'s scope. The assignment `a = 10` overwrites the local `a`, leaving the global `a` completely untouched.
`var` is function/globally scoped. By the time the asynchronous `setTimeout` callbacks run, the loop has completed and the single shared `i` variable is `3`.
The inner `let x = 20` is hoisted to the top of the IIFE block, shadowing the outer `x`. However, accessing it before initialization results in a Temporal Dead Zone `ReferenceError`.
The arrow function captures `this` lexically from `greet`'s execution context. Since `greet` was called on `obj`, `this` remains `obj` inside the arrow function, even when executed later.
Passing `user.printName` as a reference strips its object context. When `setTimeout` calls it later, `this` defaults to `window` (or `undefined` in strict mode).
Once a function is bound using `.bind()`, its `this` context is permanently locked. Subsequent `.bind()` calls cannot override the first binding.
Object keys are coerced to strings. Both `x` and `y` become the string `"[object Object]"`. The second assignment simply overwrites the first.

text: Which statement is true about `Object.create(null)`?

options.text: It creates an object that inherits from `Object.prototype`.
It creates a completely empty dictionary object with no prototype chain.
It creates a deep clone of `null`.

feedback: It creates an object without a `__proto__`. It won't have methods like `toString()` or `hasOwnProperty()`, making it a perfect 'pure' dictionary.
`delete` removes the property from the object/array, leaving an `empty` slot (yielding `undefined` if accessed), but it DOES NOT reindex the array or alter its `.length`.

text: What is the difference between `Object.freeze()` and `Object.seal()`?

options.text: `freeze` prevents all changes. `seal` allows modifying existing properties but prevents adding/deleting.
`seal` prevents all changes. `freeze` allows modifying existing properties.

feedback: `Object.seal()` seals the object structure (no new/deleted keys) but leaves existing values writable. `Object.freeze()` makes existing values read-only as well. Both are shallow.
If a constructor explicitly returns a non-primitive *object*, the `new` operator will return that object instead of the newly constructed `this` instance.
Synchronous `C` runs first. The Microtask `B` (Promise) runs immediately after the sync stack clears. The Macrotask `A` (setTimeout) runs last.

text:
What is logged?
```javascript
Promise.resolve(1)
  .then(() => { throw new Error('ops'); })
  .catch(e => 2)
  .then(res => console.log(res));
```
feedback: The `.catch()` block handles the error and returns `2`. Returning a standard value from a catch block resolves the *next* chained Promise with that value.
If the value is not a Promise, `await` automatically wraps it in a resolved Promise (`Promise.resolve(5)`) and continues execution in the next microtask.
Array `.forEach` is strictly synchronous and does not `await` its callbacks. It fires off all three async functions and immediately moves to log `'Done'`.

text: What happens if a Promise rejects and there is NO `.catch()` block attached?

options.text: It throws an 'Unhandled Promise Rejection' error.
It falls back to the global `window.onerror` handler.

feedback: Unhandled rejections trigger an UnhandledPromiseRejection warning/error. In modern Node.js, this will crash the process by default.
In nested destructuring, `a:` acts strictly as a *path* to access `b`. It does not create a variable named `a`. Only `b` is declared.
Arrow functions do NOT have their own `arguments` object. They lexically inherit `arguments` from the closest regular enclosing function (`outer`), where it is `1`.

text: What is the difference between Nullish Coalescing `??` and Logical OR `||`?

options.text: `??` falls back on all falsy values. `||` falls back only on null/undefined.
`??` falls back only on `null` or `undefined`. `||` falls back on ALL falsy values (like `0` or `""`).
`??` is for numbers, `||` is for booleans.

feedback: Using `0 || 5` results in `5`. Using `0 ?? 5` results in `0`, which is crucial when `0` is a valid, intended value.
`g.next()` executes the generator up to the first `yield` and returns an object: `{value: 1, done: false}`. Accessing `.value` gives `1`.
The first argument to a tag function is an array of the literal string segments. The string before the first expression is `"Hello "`.

text: A classic gotcha: What does `['1', '7', '11'].map(parseInt)` return?
feedback: `.map` passes `(value, index)`. `parseInt` takes `(string, radix)`. It executes `parseInt('1', 0)`, `parseInt('7', 1)` -> NaN, `parseInt('11', 2)` -> binary 3.

text: Which keyword provides a standardized way to access the global object across all environments (Browser, Node.js, Web Workers)?
feedback: ES2020 introduced `globalThis` to resolve the fragmentation (`window` in browsers, `global` in Node, `self` in Workers).

text: Why does `0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3` evaluate to `false`?

options.text: Due to base-2 binary floating-point precision (IEEE 754), it evaluates to `0.30000000000000004`.
Because `0.3` is an integer and `0.1` is a float.

feedback: Not unique to JS! Any language using IEEE 754 double-precision floats cannot perfectly represent certain decimal fractions.

text: What is the difference between `Symbol('a')` and `Symbol.for('a')`?

options.text: `Symbol()` creates a local symbol, `Symbol.for()` checks the global symbol registry and reuses it if found.
`Symbol.for()` returns a string, `Symbol()` returns a Symbol.

feedback: `Symbol('a') === Symbol('a')` is false. However, `Symbol.for('a') === Symbol.for('a')` is true because it utilizes the global registry.

text: What do the unary operators yield here: `+true` and `!"Hello"`?
feedback: The `+` unary operator coerces to Number (`+true` is `1`). The `!` operator coerces to Boolean and inverts it (`!"Hello"` is `false` because non-empty strings are truthy).

text: What is the key limitation of the keys in a `WeakMap`?

options.text: Keys must be Objects or Symbols, preventing garbage collection blockers.
WeakMaps cannot have keys, only values.

feedback: Primitive values cannot be keys in a WeakMap. Keys must be garbage-collectable references (Objects or non-registered Symbols in ES2023).

text:
What is the output?
```javascript
console.log((function(a, b, c = 1, d) {}).length);
```
feedback: A function's `.length` property indicates the number of parameters expected *before* the first parameter with a default value. `a` and `b` make it `2`.

text: What does `Object.is(-0, 0)` evaluate to?
feedback: While `-0 === 0` is `true`, `Object.is()` is slightly stricter. It accurately distinguishes `-0` from `0` (and correctly asserts `NaN` is `NaN`).

text: What is the difference between `Object.keys()` and the `for...in` loop?

options.text: `Object.keys()` returns an array, `for...in` returns a string.
`for...in` iterates over inherited enumerable properties up the prototype chain. `Object.keys()` only returns *own* enumerable properties.

feedback: This is a critical distinction. `for...in` will traverse the prototype chain unless guarded with `.hasOwnProperty()`. `Object.keys()` inherently ignores prototypes.

text: How does the global `isNaN()` differ from `Number.isNaN()`?

options.text: Global `isNaN()` coerces the argument to a Number first. `Number.isNaN()` strictly checks if the value is precisely `NaN`.

feedback: `isNaN("hello")` is `true` (coerced to NaN). `Number.isNaN("hello")` is `false` (it's a string, not the NaN type).
`var` ignores block scope, meaning `x` leaks out of the `catch` block. `y` (using let) and `e` are block-scoped and destroyed.

options.text: Yes, because `const` only protects the reference to the array, not its contents.
Yes, but it creates a new array.

feedback: Deep mutation is perfectly fine with `const`. The variable `arr` still points to the exact same memory address.

text: In a JavaScript `class`, what does `this` refer to inside a `static` method?
feedback: Static methods are called on the class construct itself (e.g., `Math.random()`), not on instantiated objects. Thus, `this` is the class.
Array methods like `map`, `forEach`, and `filter` automatically skip 'empty slots' in sparse arrays. The slot remains empty.

text: What is `typeof undeclaredVariable`?
feedback: `typeof` is a special operator. It is the only safe way to reference a completely undeclared variable without the engine throwing a ReferenceError.

text: What is the output of `Object.prototype.toString.call(null)`?
feedback: While `typeof null` incorrectly returns `'object'`, calling the base `toString` method directly reveals its true internal class: `[object Null]`.

text: In an `async` function, is `return await promise` different from `return promise`?

options.text: No, they compile to the exact same bytecode.
Yes. `return await` ensures the function waits for resolution and catches errors locally before returning. `return promise` passes the promise out immediately.
`return await` returns a primitive, `return promise` returns a Promise.

feedback: If you just `return promise`, a local `try/catch` block surrounding the return will NOT catch errors, because the rejection happens outside the function.

text: Are ES6 `import` statements hoisted?

options.text: Yes, they are statically analyzed and hoisted to the top of the module.
Only if placed inside a function.
No, they throw an error if used before declaration.

feedback: JS modules undergo a static analysis phase. `import` statements are hoisted, meaning you can technically import at the bottom of a file.

text: If you execute `let myVar = 10;` in the global scope of a browser console, what is `window.myVar`?
feedback: Unlike `var` declarations, `let` and `const` declared in the global scope DO NOT attach themselves as properties to the global `window` object.

text: Is `setTimeout("console.log(1)", 1000)` valid JavaScript?

options.text: No, the first argument must be a function.
Yes. `setTimeout` can accept a string which it evaluates like `eval()`. (Though highly discouraged).

feedback: It works, but passing strings to setTimeout is considered a severe security and performance anti-pattern (similar to `eval`).

text: How does `Promise.all([a(), b()])` handle execution time compared to `await a(); await b();`?

options.text: They take the exact same amount of time.
`Promise.all` runs them concurrently, taking the time of the longest promise. Sequential `await` runs one after another, summing their times.
Sequential `await` is faster because it saves memory.

feedback: If `a()` takes 2s and `b()` takes 3s: `Promise.all` finishes in ~3s. Sequential `await` finishes in ~5s.

text: What happens when you run `obj.method?.()` and `method` does not exist?

options.text: Throws `TypeError: obj.method is not a function`
Evaluates to `undefined` without throwing an error.

feedback: Optional chaining works for function calls too! It short-circuits and returns `undefined` if the property before `?.()` is null/undefined.

text: What is `typeof function() {}`?
feedback: While functions are technically objects in JavaScript, the `typeof` operator has a special carve-out that specifically returns `"function"`.

text: Which of the following is NOT an Iterable by default in JavaScript?
feedback: Plain objects do not have a default `Symbol.iterator`. You cannot use `for...of` directly on them without `Object.keys()` or similar.
